title: Microlepia aculeata
taxonomy: Microlepia aculeata is a species of plant in the family Dryopteridaceae, commonly known as the wood ferns. The family Dryopteridaceae belongs to the order Polypodiales, which includes other types of ferns. This order is part of the class Polypodiopsida, which is a division of vascular plants. The class Polypodiopsida is part of the phylum Pteridophyta, which includes all ferns and fern allies. The phylum Pteridophyta is also known as the vascular cryptogams, which are plants that reproduce via spores but have vascular tissue.
uses: Microlepia aculeata, a species of fern, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. Its leaves are said to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help in treating wounds and skin conditions. In some cultures, the plant is also used to treat fever, cough, and respiratory issues. However, more research is needed to fully understand the medicinal uses and potential benefits of Microlepia aculeata.

intro: Microlepia aculeata, also known as the thorny rake fern, is a species of fern native to tropical Asia. This plant has been traditionally used in various cultures for its medicinal properties, exhibiting potential health benefits such as anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, which may aid in the management of conditions like arthritis and cancer. The bioactive constituents of Microlepia aculeata include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and saponins, which are responsible for its pharmacological activities. In traditional medicine, Microlepia aculeata is commonly prepared as a decoction or an extract, often used to treat various ailments, including fever, cough, and respiratory issues.
